articles.title: Earth may return as a supercontinent with extreme heat - Earth.com
articles.search_term: (heatwave OR "heat wave" OR "severe heatwave" OR "extreme heat" OR "heat spell" OR "scorching heat" OR "blistering heat" OR "sweltering heat" OR "hot winds" OR loo OR "hot loo winds" OR "intense heat" OR "unbearable heat" OR "deadly heat") Assam
articles.full_text:
New projections show that in about 250 million years, today’s continents could merge into one vast landmass, and France would sit near its cooler outer edge.
The same research finds that intense heat and widespread dryness would shrink the areas where mammals could survive, pushing life toward the edges of that future continent.
Maps erase oceans
On a future world atlas, the Atlantic shrinks away and France sits near the junction of Europe and Africa.
From that geography, the University of Bristol ran climate simulations that tracked where mammals could shed heat.
Linking the map to animal cooling limits, Dr. Alexander Farnsworth in southwest England translated plate motion into a habitability forecast.
Soon after, basic geology matters most, because plates drive the collisions that close seas and build the next continent.
Plates pull together
Under every ocean, Earth’s crust breaks into tectonic plates, huge slabs of rock that creep just a few inches each year.
As ocean crust cools, it grows denser and sinks at subduction zones, long trenches where one plate dives downward.
That sinking edge pulls the rest along, so continents drift until collisions stitch land together and squeeze out inland seas.
Over time, this crust recycling erases old oceans, so world maps stay as temporary snapshots rather than permanent borders.
France’s future position
By the end of the continental merge, France rides northward, putting its coastline nearer the Arctic Circle than before.
Closed seas follow the collisions, and the Mediterranean basin disappears when Africa and Europe grind together.
Morocco, Algeria, and Tunisia sit on the same continuous landmass as France in that layout, not across water.
Without the Mediterranean, France loses a nearby heat sink, so summers grow harsher and rain patterns change.
Heat stress rises
Computer models in a paper projected the supercontinent climate and found heat stress could wipe out most land mammals.
Farnsworth later described the projected conditions in simple terms, saying the future supercontinent would be extremely difficult for mammals to survive. In his view, much of that land would become too hot and dry to support large animal life.
Once wet-bulb temperature – the combined effect of heat and humidity – climbs too high, sweating fails and even shade stops helping.
Volcanoes raise CO2
During supercontinent assembly, new cracks and long volcanic chains boost outgassing, the release of gases from molten rock.
Extra carbon dioxide traps more heat near the surface, and that greenhouse effect builds for thousands of years at a time.
Alongside those gases, the Sun grows 2.5% brighter, adding energy that raises baseline temperatures even before feedback kicks in.
In that combination, heat stress rises faster than animals can adapt, because evolution rarely outruns planetary-scale change.
Big continents bake
Far from any coastline, the interior experiences continentality, bigger temperature swings away from the ocean.
Dry air follows, because moisture drops out before winds can cross thousands of miles of hot land.
Across much of the merged continent, models put summer heat above 104°F (40°C) for long stretches each year.
For animals that cool by breathing or sweating, that inland heat means a shrinking window for activity, feeding, and reproduction.
Coastal refuges remain
Near coasts and high latitudes, ocean air limits extremes and keeps water cycling through clouds, rivers, and soils.
Northern France lands close to that more forgiving band, so its future importance comes from climate, not politics.
Britain, Portugal, and parts of North Africa also stay nearer the cooler edge, though their coastlines look nothing like today.
Outside those margins, deserts and heat zones dominate the supercontinent, and large land animals become rare across the interior.
Models have limits
Even the best forecasts rest on assumptions, because no one can watch plates move across deep time.
Different collision paths could build a very different supercontinent, and a small change early can compound into a new map.
Missing details also matter, including lakes, narrow straits, and ice sheets that can cool regions and soften dry spells.
Within those limits, the projections still highlight a simple rule: land far from oceans heats up first and stays hot longest.
Lessons for today
Long-range experiments like this help scientists test climate tools under strange conditions, then improve how models handle heat and water.
Planet hunters also borrow these ideas, because a world can sit in the right orbit yet still become too hot inland.
Geology controls where land and oceans sit, while gases and sunlight control how hot and dry those places become.
France’s importance in this forecast comes from latitude and coastline, not any enduring national boundary.
What the maps imply
A planet that reunites its continents also rewrites where cooling and water can reach, making geography decide which areas stay livable.
Future work can tighten these forecasts by adding more Earth details, while keeping clear that the far future stays uncertain.
The study is published in Nature.
